title: Meal planning as a food waste reduction tool
subtitle: A structured approach to buying and using food before it spoils
excerpt: The UK wastes 9.5 million tonnes of food per year, of which 70 percent comes from households. A household of four wastes an average of 700 pounds of food per year. Most of this waste is avoidable: it comes from buying more than is needed and not using what is already in the kitchen. A simple weekly plan that starts with what is already in the fridge and cupboards cuts this waste by 30 to 40 percent in the first month.
sourceNotes: WRAP Household food waste in the UK (2021). Love Food Hate Waste UK guidance. WRAP consumer behaviour change evidence summary. Food Standards Agency guidance on date labels and storage.

content.text: Most household food waste falls into four categories: fresh vegetables and salads that go soft, bread that goes mouldy, fresh meat or fish not used in time, and leftovers not eaten. Each has a different fix. Meal planning addresses all four by making a deliberate decision in advance about what will be eaten and when.
Once a week, check what is already in the fridge, freezer, and cupboards. Note anything that needs using within the next 3 to 4 days.
Plan meals around these items first. A meal plan that starts from what you already have cuts food waste more than any shopping strategy.
Plan meals for 5 to 6 days (not 7). Leave 1 to 2 days flexible for leftovers, eating out, or something that runs over.
Write the shopping list from the meal plan. Buy only what is on the list, with small additions for one-off fresh items (fruit for snacking, salad ingredients).
High-waste categories and how to manage them
Vegetables and salad: plan at least two meals using fresh vegetables in the first half of the week. By mid-week, use softer items in cooked dishes (soups, stews, stir-fry) rather than raw salads.
Bread: freeze bread on the day of purchase if it will not be finished in 2 days. Frozen bread toasts from frozen. Reduce loaf size if half a loaf routinely goes mouldy.
Meat and fish: plan these for the first 1 to 2 days after shopping. Freeze on the day of purchase if not needed until later in the week.

Leftovers: build a deliberate leftovers meal into the plan (Friday lunch from Thursday dinner, for example). Cooked grains, roasted vegetables, and cooked pulses last 3 days in the fridge and form the basis of quick lunches.
Use by: a safety date. Do not eat after this date. Applies to perishable products (fresh meat, fish, some dairy). Eating food past a use-by date is a food safety risk.

Best before: a quality date. The food is safe to eat after this date but may be past its best flavour or texture. Dry pasta, tinned goods, and frozen food carry best-before dates. Eating past a best-before is a quality question, not a safety question.
